title: Duxbury, Massachusetts

infobox.value: (alternative older spellings: "Duxborough" and "Duxboro"[[1]](#cite_note-1)) is a town in [Plymouth County, Massachusetts](/wiki/Plymouth_County,_Massachusetts "Plymouth County, Massachusetts"), United States. A suburb located on the [South Shore](/wiki/South_Shore_(Massachusetts) "South Shore (Massachusetts)") approximately 35 miles (56 km) to the southeast of [Boston](/wiki/Boston "Boston"), the population was 16,090 at the 2020 census.[[2]](#cite_note-2) The town contains the census-designated places (CDPs) of [Duxbury](/wiki/Duxbury_(CDP),_Massachusetts "Duxbury (CDP), Massachusetts"), [Green Harbor](/wiki/Green_Harbor,_Massachusetts "Green Harbor, Massachusetts"), and [South Duxbury](/wiki/South_Duxbury,_Massachusetts "South Duxbury, Massachusetts").

**Duxbury** (alternative older spellings: "Duxborough" and "Duxboro"[[1]](#cite_note-1)) is a town in [Plymouth County, Massachusetts](/wiki/Plymouth_County,_Massachusetts "Plymouth County, Massachusetts"), United States. A suburb located on the [South Shore](/wiki/South_Shore_(Massachusetts) "South Shore (Massachusetts)") approximately 35 miles (56 km) to the southeast of [Boston](/wiki/Boston "Boston"), the population was 16,090 at the 2020 census.[[2]](#cite_note-2) The town contains the census-designated places (CDPs) of [Duxbury](/wiki/Duxbury_(CDP),_Massachusetts "Duxbury (CDP), Massachusetts"), [Green Harbor](/wiki/Green_Harbor,_Massachusetts "Green Harbor, Massachusetts"), and [South Duxbury](/wiki/South_Duxbury,_Massachusetts "South Duxbury, Massachusetts").

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Duxbury,_Massachusetts&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/b/b6/John_Alden_House.jpg)](/wiki/File:John_Alden_House.jpg)

[John Alden House](/wiki/John_Alden_House "John Alden House"), built c. 1700

The area now known as Duxbury was inhabited by people as early as 12,000 to 9,000 BCE. By the time [European](/wiki/Europe "Europe") settlers arrived there, the region was inhabited by the [Wampanoags](/wiki/Wampanoag_(tribe) "Wampanoag (tribe)"), who called it Mattakeesett, meaning "place of many fish."[[3]](#cite_note-DuxburyInBrief-3)

### 17th century

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Duxbury,_Massachusetts&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: 17th century")]

In 1620, [English](/wiki/English_(people) "English (people)") settlers known as the [Pilgrims](/wiki/Pilgrim_(Plymouth_Colony) "Pilgrim (Plymouth Colony)") established their colony in nearby [Plymouth](/wiki/Plymouth,_Massachusetts "Plymouth, Massachusetts"). Per the terms of their contract with financial backers in [London](/wiki/London "London"), they were required to live together in a tight community for seven years. At the end of that term in 1627, land along the coast was allotted to settlers for [farming](/wiki/Farming "Farming"). Thus, the coastline from Plymouth to [Marshfield](/wiki/Marshfield,_Massachusetts "Marshfield, Massachusetts"), including Duxbury, likely named after [Myles Standish's](/wiki/Myles_Standish "Myles Standish") ancestral home of [Duxbury Hall](/wiki/Duxbury_Hall "Duxbury Hall") in [Chorley](/wiki/Chorley "Chorley"), was parceled out, and many settlers began moving away from Plymouth.[[3]](#cite_note-DuxburyInBrief-3)

At first, those who settled in Duxbury came to work their new farms just in the warmer months and returned to Plymouth during the winter. It was not long, however, before they began to build homes on their land, and soon requested permission from the colony to be set off as a separate community with their own church. Duxbury, which originally included land that is now [Pembroke](/wiki/Pembroke,_Massachusetts "Pembroke, Massachusetts"), was incorporated in 1637.[[3]](#cite_note-DuxburyInBrief-3)

Some of the most influential men in the colony received grants in Duxbury and became its first leaders. Captain [Myles Standish](/wiki/Myles_Standish "Myles Standish"), the military leader of the colony, lived in "the Nook," an area now known as Standish Shore. Elder [William Brewster](/wiki/William_Brewster_(Pilgrim) "William Brewster (Pilgrim)") was for many years the religious leader of the colony, in which he led services to the colony until it received its own minister in 1637. [John Alden](/wiki/John_Alden_(Pilgrim) "John Alden (Pilgrim)") was another important settler. His house, now a museum on Alden Street, was the site of many important meetings of the colony's leaders.

The graves of some of Duxbury's first settlers can be found in the [Old Burying Ground](/wiki/Myles_Standish_Burial_Ground "Myles Standish Burial Ground") on Chestnut Street, next to the site of the original [meetinghouse](/wiki/Meetinghouse "Meetinghouse").[[3]](#cite_note-DuxburyInBrief-3)

Theory has it that the town was named by Myles Standish after the family estate of his childhood in [Lancashire](/wiki/Lancashire "Lancashire"). The ancient [Standish family](/wiki/Standish_family "Standish family") in northern England owned much land and large estates, including the two main family headquarters of [Standish Hall](/wiki/Standish_Hall "Standish Hall") and [Duxbury Manor](/wiki/Duxbury_Hall "Duxbury Hall"), in Lancashire, since before the Middle Ages. Myles Standish's will delineates his inheritance rights to very particular lands near and around Standish and mostly Duxbury Manor, stating his descent from both lines of the Standish family; and so it has been suggested that he named the new town in Massachusetts after the estate where he grew up.[[3]](#cite_note-DuxburyInBrief-3)

Duxbury was primarily a farming community throughout the 17th and 18th centuries.

### 18th century

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Duxbury,_Massachusetts&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: 18th century")]

Its quiet history in the 18th century was interrupted only by the [Revolutionary War](/wiki/American_Revolutionary_War "American Revolutionary War").[[3]](#cite_note-DuxburyInBrief-3) In the years leading up to the Revolutionary War, opposition to the [British](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Great_Britain "Kingdom of Great Britain") was quite fierce in Duxbury, with crowds meeting regularly at Captain's Hill to burn effigies of British officials in protest of the [Stamp Act](/wiki/Stamp_Act "Stamp Act").

In 1775, [General Thomas Gage](/wiki/Thomas_Gage "Thomas Gage") dispatched a company of regulars to the town in response to pleas from the loyalists at [Marshfield](/wiki/Marshfield,_Massachusetts "Marshfield, Massachusetts").[[4]](#cite_note-4)

When the [Minuteman](/wiki/Minuteman "Minuteman") alarm sounded on April 19, 1775, with news of the [Battles of Lexington and Concord](/wiki/Battles_of_Lexington_and_Concord "Battles of Lexington and Concord"), many volunteers mustered to the regiment of Colonel Theophilus Cotton from [Plymouth](/wiki/Plymouth,_Massachusetts "Plymouth, Massachusetts"), [Kingston](/wiki/Kingston,_Massachusetts "Kingston, Massachusetts"), and Duxbury, and headed for Marshfield to engage the British. The colonial officers held a council of war at the home of Lt. Col. Briggs Alden in Duxbury.[[5]](#cite_note-5)

Duxbury's [shipbuilding](/wiki/Shipbuilding "Shipbuilding") era began immediately after the end of the [Revolutionary War](/wiki/American_Revolutionary_War "American Revolutionary War") with the [Treaty of Paris](/wiki/Treaty_of_Paris_(1783) "Treaty of Paris (1783)"). The new nation was granted [fishing](/wiki/Fishing "Fishing") rights on the [Grand Banks](/wiki/Grand_Banks "Grand Banks"), and several families took advantage of the new opportunity and began to build large fishing [schooners](/wiki/Schooner "Schooner"). The schooners built in the 1790s gave way to larger [brigs](/wiki/Brig "Brig") and eventually three-masted [ships](/wiki/Ship "Ship"). As several [merchant](/wiki/Merchant "Merchant") families began to amass large fleets, [shipyards](/wiki/Shipyard "Shipyard") and other ancillary industries flourished and Duxbury prospered. By the 1840s, Duxbury boasted about 20 shipyards and produced an average of ten large sailing vessels per year.[[3]](#cite_note-DuxburyInBrief-3)

The largest industry in Duxbury was owned by Ezra Weston, who came to be known as "King Caesar" due to his success and influence. Weston began building small vessels in 1764 and soon became famous for his successful merchant fleet. His son, [Ezra Weston II](/wiki/Ezra_Weston_II "Ezra Weston II"), who inherited his father's kingly sobriquet, would bring the industry to its height.

### 19th century

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Duxbury,_Massachusetts&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: 19th century")]

[Lloyd's of London](/wiki/Lloyd%27s_of_London "Lloyd's of London") recognized Weston as the owner of the largest fleet in [America](/wiki/Americas "Americas"), and this judgment was confirmed by [Daniel Webster](/wiki/Daniel_Webster "Daniel Webster") in a speech in 1841. His empire, a forerunner of [vertical integration](/wiki/Vertical_integration "Vertical integration"), dominated the town.[[3]](#cite_note-DuxburyInBrief-3) The [King Caesar House](/wiki/King_Caesar_House "King Caesar House") is now a museum owned by the [Duxbury Rural and Historical Society](/wiki/Duxbury_Rural_and_Historical_Society "Duxbury Rural and Historical Society").[[6]](#cite_note-6) By 1837, there were nearly 40 [shipyards](/wiki/Shipyard "Shipyard") along the west shore of [Duxbury Bay](/wiki/Duxbury_Bay_(Massachusetts) "Duxbury Bay (Massachusetts)") between Captains Hill and the mouth of the [Bluefish River](/wiki/Bluefish_River_(Massachusetts) "Bluefish River (Massachusetts)"). These shipyards were supported by a [foundry](/wiki/Foundry "Foundry"), a [sail loft](/wiki/Loft#Sail_loft "Loft"), a [ropewalk](/wiki/Ropewalk "Ropewalk"), and a [spar](/wiki/Spar_(sailing) "Spar (sailing)") soak where masts were stored in a pool of salt water to prevent [warping](/wiki/Wood_warping "Wood warping") before they were stepped as ships' masts. Inland farmers provided food for the ships' crews; and a [bank](/wiki/Bank "Bank") handled the shipbuilders' money.[[7]](#cite_note-7)

Demand for shallow-[draft](/wiki/Draft_(hull) "Draft (hull)") ships declined by the 1850s. Duxbury Bay was too shallow to build the deep-draft [clippers](/wiki/Clipper "Clipper") and [steamships](/wiki/Steamships "Steamships") dominating oceanic trade and [railroads](/wiki/Rail_transport "Rail transport") were competing for the coastal trade. While other Massachusetts towns grew, Duxbury went into a long economic decline.[[3]](#cite_note-DuxburyInBrief-3)

There was, however, a silver lining. By the 1870s, Duxbury's rural character and unspoiled [bay](/wiki/Bay "Bay") began to attract summer visitors. Duxbury soon gained a reputation as an idyllic [summer resort](/wiki/Summer_resort "Summer resort"). With the 1871 completion of the Duxbury & Cohasset Railroad,[[8]](#cite_note-8) large numbers of city-folk from Boston could pay $1.50 for a round-trip ticket and enjoy Duxbury's refreshing environment. [Boarding houses](/wiki/Boarding_house "Boarding house") sprang up everywhere. The Miles Standish Hotel on the Nook soon became enormously popular. The Myles Standish monument, completed in 1898, was a result of this tourist influx.[[3]](#cite_note-DuxburyInBrief-3)

### 20th century

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Duxbury,_Massachusetts&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: 20th century")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/aa/House_flag_of_the_Winsor_Line.svg/330px-House_flag_of_the_Winsor_Line.svg.png)](/wiki/File:House_flag_of_the_Winsor_Line.svg)

The [Maritime House flag](/wiki/Maritime_flag "Maritime flag") of the Winsor Line, managed by Nathaniel Winsor III, continued in operation until 1907.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d6/North_Side_of_Powder_Point%2C_Duxbury%2C_MA.jpg/330px-North_Side_of_Powder_Point%2C_Duxbury%2C_MA.jpg)](/wiki/File:North_Side_of_Powder_Point,_Duxbury,_MA.jpg)

Duxbury's beach and residences, c. 1910

This pattern continued in Duxbury well into the 20th century. It was not until the construction of [Route 3](/wiki/Massachusetts_Route_3 "Massachusetts Route 3") that transportation to Boston became expedient and the town's population rose further with the arrival of thousands of year-round residents.[[3]](#cite_note-DuxburyInBrief-3)

## Geography

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Duxbury,_Massachusetts&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Geography")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e2/Duxbury_Bay.jpg/330px-Duxbury_Bay.jpg)](/wiki/File:Duxbury_Bay.jpg)

The [Bluefish River](/wiki/Bluefish_River_(Massachusetts) "Bluefish River (Massachusetts)") inlet with [King Caesar House](/wiki/King_Caesar_House "King Caesar House") in the background

According to the [United States Census Bureau](/wiki/United_States_Census_Bureau "United States Census Bureau"), the town has a total area of 37.6 square miles (97 km2), of which 23.8 square miles (62 km2) is land and 13.9 square miles (36 km2) (36.87%) is water. Duxbury is bordered by [Cape Cod Bay](/wiki/Cape_Cod_Bay "Cape Cod Bay") to the east, [Duxbury Bay](/wiki/Duxbury_Bay_(Massachusetts) "Duxbury Bay (Massachusetts)"), Kingston Bay, and [Plymouth](/wiki/Plymouth,_Massachusetts "Plymouth, Massachusetts") to the southeast, [Kingston](/wiki/Kingston,_Massachusetts "Kingston, Massachusetts") to the southwest, [Pembroke](/wiki/Pembroke,_Massachusetts "Pembroke, Massachusetts") to the west and northwest, and [Marshfield](/wiki/Marshfield,_Massachusetts "Marshfield, Massachusetts") to the north. The town's border with Plymouth is due to the town's having the only land access to Saquish Neck, a thin, hook-shaped strip of land along Duxbury Bay, at the tip of which is [Saquish Head](/wiki/Saquish_Head "Saquish Head") in Plymouth.

Duxbury is the sixth largest [cranberry](/wiki/Cranberry "Cranberry") producer in [Massachusetts](/wiki/Massachusetts "Massachusetts") and has [oyster](/wiki/Oyster "Oyster") beds and other [shellfish](/wiki/Shellfish "Shellfish").

The town has many ponds and bogs throughout. The Back River lies along the western edge of Saquish Neck, and has many tributaries from the local rivers, brooks, and marshes. There are several sanctuaries, a conservation area and other forests within the town, especially in the western half.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

As of the [census](/wiki/Census "Census")[[20]](#cite_note-GR2-20) of 2010, there were 15,059 people, 5,344 households, and 4,162 families residing in the town. The population density was 632.7 inhabitants per square mile (244.3/km2). There were 5,875 housing units at an average density of 246.7 per square mile (95.3/km2). The racial makeup of the town was 98.0% [White](/wiki/White_(U.S._Census) "White (U.S. Census)"), 0.60% [Black](/wiki/African_American_(U.S._Census) "African American (U.S. Census)") or [African American](/wiki/Race_(United_States_Census) "Race (United States Census)"), 0.30% [Native American](/wiki/Native_American_(U.S._Census) "Native American (U.S. Census)"), 1.40% [Asian](/wiki/Asian_(U.S._Census) "Asian (U.S. Census)"), 0.10% [Pacific Islander](/wiki/Pacific_Islander_(U.S._Census) "Pacific Islander (U.S. Census)"), 0.50% from [other races](/wiki/Race_(United_States_Census) "Race (United States Census)"), and 0.80% from two or more races. [Hispanic](/wiki/Hispanic_(U.S._Census) "Hispanic (U.S. Census)") or [Latino](/wiki/Latino_(U.S._Census) "Latino (U.S. Census)") of any race were 1.20% of the population.

There were 5,344 households, of which 38.7% had children under the age of 18 living with them, 68.1% were [married couples](/wiki/Marriage "Marriage") living together, 7.4% had a female householder with no husband present, and 22.1% were non-families. 19.5% of all households were made up of individuals, and 11.8% had someone living alone who was 65 years of age or older. The average household size was 2.79 and the average family size was 3.23.

In the town, the population was spread out, with 22.6% under the age of 15; 7.6% from 15 to 19; 5.3% from 20 to 29; 7.0% from 30 to 39; 17.5% from 40 to 49; 23.7% from 50 to 64 and 16.3% who were 65 years of age or older. The median age was 44.8 years. For every 100 females, there were 92.6 males. For every 100 females age 18 and over, there were 88.5 males.

The median income for a household in the town was $117,124; and the median income for a family was $136,245. Males had a median income of $77,228 versus $41,730 for females. The [per capita income](/wiki/Per_capita_income "Per capita income") for the town was $50,242. About 1.2% of families and 2.3% of the population were below the [poverty line](/wiki/Poverty_line "Poverty line"), including 3.1% of those under age 18 and 3.1% of those age 65 or over.

## Government

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Duxbury,_Massachusetts&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Government")]

On the national level, Duxbury is a part of [Massachusetts's 9th congressional district](/wiki/Massachusetts%27s_9th_congressional_district "Massachusetts's 9th congressional district"), and is currently represented by [Bill Keating](/wiki/William_R._Keating "William R. Keating"). The state's senior ([Class II](/wiki/Classes_of_United_States_senators#Class_II "Classes of United States senators")) member of the [United States Senate](/wiki/United_States_Senate "United States Senate"), elected in 2012, is [Elizabeth Warren](/wiki/Elizabeth_Warren "Elizabeth Warren"). The junior ([Class I](/wiki/Classes_of_United_States_senators#Class_I "Classes of United States senators")) senator is [Ed Markey](/wiki/Ed_Markey "Ed Markey").

On the state level, Duxbury is represented in the [Massachusetts House of Representatives](/wiki/Massachusetts_House_of_Representatives "Massachusetts House of Representatives") as a part of the Sixth Plymouth district; the Sixth includes the Town of Duxbury, and portions of the Towns of Hanson, Halifax, Marshfield, and Pembroke. The town is represented in the [Massachusetts Senate](/wiki/Massachusetts_Senate "Massachusetts Senate") as a part of the [Plymouth and Norfolk district](/wiki/Massachusetts_Senate%27s_Plymouth_and_Norfolk_district "Massachusetts Senate's Plymouth and Norfolk district"), which includes the towns of Cohasset, Duxbury, Hingham, Hull, Marshfield, Norwell, Scituate and Weymouth.[[21]](#cite_note-21) The town is patrolled by the First (Norwell) Barracks of Troop D of the [Massachusetts State Police](/wiki/Massachusetts_State_Police "Massachusetts State Police").[[22]](#cite_note-22)

Duxbury is governed by the [open town meeting](/wiki/Open_town_meeting "Open town meeting") form of government, and is led by a [town manager](/wiki/Town_manager "Town manager") and a [board of selectmen](/wiki/Board_of_selectmen "Board of selectmen"). The town operates its own police and fire departments, with the police station and central fire station located in the southeast and additional fire station in the northwest parts of town. There are two post offices in town; one is at Hall's Corner (near Goose Point) and the other is at Snug Harbor, along Duxbury Bay just south of Powder Point.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

The Duxbury Free Library is located in the heart of town, next to the [John Alden House](/wiki/John_Alden_House "John Alden House"), and is a member of the Old Colony Library Network. The town also has a highway department, located behind the Town Hall, and a harbormaster, whose office is located next to the Duxbury Yacht Club near Snug Harbor. Duxbury is located within ten miles (16 km) of the [Pilgrim Nuclear Power Station](/wiki/Pilgrim_Nuclear_Generating_Station "Pilgrim Nuclear Generating Station"), and as such has a well-organized emergency management agency. The nearest hospitals are [Jordan Hospital](/wiki/Jordan_Hospital_(Massachusetts) "Jordan Hospital (Massachusetts)") in [Plymouth](/wiki/Plymouth,_Massachusetts "Plymouth, Massachusetts"), South Shore Hospital in [Weymouth](/wiki/Weymouth,_Massachusetts "Weymouth, Massachusetts"), and Brockton Hospital.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

## Education

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Duxbury,_Massachusetts&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Education")]

### Public schools

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Duxbury,_Massachusetts&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Public schools")]

Over $26 million of Duxbury's annual budget is devoted to the town's nationally recognized public school system. According to *[Newsweek](/wiki/Newsweek "Newsweek")* magazine's 2005, 2006, and 2007 rankings of the Nation's Top 1200 Public High Schools (the top 5% of public school systems), Duxbury was ranked at 246, 185, and 142 respectively. By 2013, it had fallen off of the *Newsweek* national rankings and locally, *Boston Magazine*'s yearly town/school rankings, dropped it out of a top-40 spot.[[24]](#cite_note-24)

Duxbury operates its own school system for the town's approximately 3,400 students, serving preschool through 12th grade. Chandler School is located near Tree of Knowledge Corner in the west of town and serves students from pre-kindergarten through second grade. The Alden Elementary School, near the John Alden House, serves grades 3–5.

Duxbury Middle/[High School](/wiki/Duxbury_High_School "Duxbury High School") is located on Alden Street and serves grades 6–12. Duxbury High School[[25]](#cite_note-auto2-25) was rebuilt into a combined building along with Duxbury Middle School,[[25]](#cite_note-auto2-25) called the 21st Learning experience. Duxbury's athletic teams are known as the Dragons, and their colors are green and white. Their chief rival is [Marshfield High School](/wiki/Marshfield_High_School_(Massachusetts) "Marshfield High School (Massachusetts)"), team mascot the Rams, and they play against them in the Thanksgiving Day Tournament. It is a local event whose rivalry goes back 30 years.

The parents of a former Duxbury Middle School student filed a $1-million lawsuit in March 2021 against the Duxbury Public Schools and a former gym teacher and hockey coach, accusing him of repeatedly raping their son in his eighth grade gym class.[[26]](#cite_note-auto1-26)[[27]](#cite_note-auto-27) The school district received a request for information from the [United States Center for SafeSport](/wiki/United_States_Center_for_SafeSport "United States Center for SafeSport").[[26]](#cite_note-auto1-26)[[27]](#cite_note-auto-27)

In March 2021, the High School's Football Coach David Maimaron was fired, despite an exceptionally strong football record, when it was exposed that the football team was using anti-Semitic language (including terms like "Auschwitz") as ["audibles"](/wiki/Audible_(American_football) "Audible (American football)") during game time.[[28]](#cite_note-28)[[29]](#cite_note-29)[[30]](#cite_note-30) As audibles must be known and agreed to by players and coaches, it was assumed that there was staff approval of seriously racially inappropriate language, and the Coach was fired and the team was disciplined.

### Private schools

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Duxbury,_Massachusetts&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Private schools")]

There are two private schools located in Duxbury. Bay Farm Montessori Academy[[31]](#cite_note-31) is a private, independent school located in the southern corner of town and serves Toddlers through grade 8. Good Shepherd Christian Academy[[32]](#cite_note-32) is a private, Christian school which serves students from kindergarten through eighth grade. The nearest private high school was the now closed [Sacred Heart](/wiki/Sacred_Heart_High_School_(Kingston,_Massachusetts) "Sacred Heart High School (Kingston, Massachusetts)") in Kingston. The town has no vocational schools.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

## Transportation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Duxbury,_Massachusetts&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Transportation")]

### Bus

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Duxbury,_Massachusetts&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Bus")]

For buses in Duxbury, the local [Greater Attleboro Taunton Regional Transit Authority](/wiki/Greater_Attleboro_Taunton_Regional_Transit_Authority "Greater Attleboro Taunton Regional Transit Authority") (GATRA) bus passes through the town on the SAIL line (Seaside Area Inter-town Link) and it stops at Halls Corner shopping district ([South Duxbury](/wiki/South_Duxbury "South Duxbury")), Island Creek, Millbrook Motors (Cox Corner), and the Duxbury Free Library (Millbrook). Another Bus Line that goes through Duxbury is the P&B Line (Plymouth & Brockton) which stops at Millbrook Motors (Cox Corner). The last bus line that goes through Duxbury is the [Greyhound Bus](/wiki/Greyhound_Bus "Greyhound Bus") Line which runs national service and into [Canada](/wiki/Canada "Canada"), which stops at Millbrook Motors (Cox Corner).

### Road

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Duxbury,_Massachusetts&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Road")]

[Route 3](/wiki/Massachusetts_Route_3 "Massachusetts Route 3"), a two-lane freeway also known as the Pilgrims Highway, passes through the town, with exits 20 and 22 granting access to the town from it. Routes [3A](/wiki/Massachusetts_Route_3A_(south) "Massachusetts Route 3A (south)"), [14](/wiki/Massachusetts_Route_14 "Massachusetts Route 14"), [53](/wiki/Massachusetts_Route_53 "Massachusetts Route 53") and [139](/wiki/Massachusetts_Route_139 "Massachusetts Route 139") also pass through the town. Routes 14 and 139 both end in the town, and Route 53 ends less than 1⁄2 mile (0.80 km) south of the town line, at its intersection with Route 3A in [Kingston](/wiki/Kingston,_Massachusetts "Kingston, Massachusetts").

### Other

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Duxbury,_Massachusetts&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Other")]

Just south of Duxbury, the nearest [MBTA Commuter Rail](/wiki/MBTA_Commuter_Rail "MBTA Commuter Rail") station is [Kingston station](/wiki/Kingston_station_(Massachusetts) "Kingston station (Massachusetts)"), not far from the town line. The nearest municipal airport is [Marshfield Municipal Airport](/wiki/Marshfield_Municipal_Airport_(Massachusetts) "Marshfield Municipal Airport (Massachusetts)") as well as [Logan International Airport](/wiki/Logan_International_Airport "Logan International Airport") in [Boston](/wiki/Boston "Boston") being the nearest national and major airport. There are no bicycle paths or bicycle lanes. A few of the roads in the town have sidewalks.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

* [Duxbury Yacht Club](/wiki/Duxbury_Yacht_Club "Duxbury Yacht Club") near Snug Harbor was founded in 1875 and incorporated in 1895. As part of the club's 120th anniversary celebrations, David A. Mittell wrote "The Duxbury Yacht Club Story."[[40]](#cite_note-40)
* [First Parish Church, Unitarian Universalist](/wiki/First_Parish_Church_(Duxbury,_Massachusetts) "First Parish Church (Duxbury, Massachusetts)"): Founded in 1632 by the [Pilgrims](/wiki/Pilgrim_(Plymouth_Colony) "Pilgrim (Plymouth Colony)"), it was the second religious body of the [Plymouth Colony](/wiki/Plymouth_Colony "Plymouth Colony").[[41]](#cite_note-41)
* [Capt. Gershom Bradford House](/wiki/Capt._Gershom_Bradford_House "Capt. Gershom Bradford House"): This early 19th-century house remains virtually untouched from when it was built and furnished by the seafaring captain. It remained in the family for four generations until donated to the Duxbury Rural and Historical Society.[[42]](#cite_note-42)
* [John Alden House](/wiki/John_Alden_House "John Alden House"), built in 1653, home to [Pilgrim](/wiki/Pilgrims_(Plymouth_Colony) "Pilgrims (Plymouth Colony)") [John Alden](/wiki/John_Alden "John Alden")
* [King Caesar House](/wiki/King_Caesar_House "King Caesar House")[[43]](#cite_note-43)
* [Myles Standish Burial Ground](/wiki/Myles_Standish_Burial_Ground "Myles Standish Burial Ground"), cemetery and Pilgrim burial site
* [Myles Standish Monument State Reservation](/wiki/Myles_Standish_Monument_State_Reservation "Myles Standish Monument State Reservation")[[44]](#cite_note-44)
* [North Hill Marsh Pond](/wiki/North_Hill_Marsh_Pond "North Hill Marsh Pond"): The Massachusetts Audubon Society's 129-acre (52 ha) sanctuary on Mayflower Street includes a forest, bike trails, and a 90-acre (36 ha) pond.[[45]](#cite_note-45)
* [Old Shipbuilder's Historic District](/wiki/Old_Shipbuilder%27s_Historic_District "Old Shipbuilder's Historic District")
* [Pillsbury Summer House](/wiki/Pillsbury_Summer_House "Pillsbury Summer House")
* [Mill Pond](/wiki/Mill_Pond_(Duxbury,_Massachusetts) "Mill Pond (Duxbury, Massachusetts)"), [Island Creek Pond](/wiki/Island_Creek_Pond "Island Creek Pond"), [Island Creek](/wiki/Island_Creek_Pond "Island Creek Pond")[[46]](#cite_note-46)
* Camp Wing: Located in Duxbury's northeast tip, it is a summer camp with many year-round events.
* [Wright Memorial Library](/wiki/Wright_Memorial_Library "Wright Memorial Library"), home of the Duxbury Student Union and the Drew Archival Library of the [Duxbury Rural and Historical Society](/wiki/Duxbury_Rural_and_Historical_Society "Duxbury Rural and Historical Society")
* [Nathaniel Winsor Jr. House](/wiki/Nathaniel_Winsor_Jr._House "Nathaniel Winsor Jr. House"), headquarters of the Duxbury Rural and Historical Society
* Powder Point Bridge: A wooden bridge built from Powder Point to [Gurnet](/wiki/Gurnet_Point,_Massachusetts "Gurnet Point, Massachusetts") in 1892. The bridge was in the [Guinness World Records](/wiki/Guinness_World_Records "Guinness World Records") as the longest and oldest wooden bridge in the world until it sustained some damage from a fire in 1985.[[47]](#cite_note-47) The fire proved that the bridge as a whole was unstable, and in 1986 it was rebuilt in its original style and is still standing today. The bridge was also shown in the 2013 film *[The Way, Way Back](/wiki/The_Way,_Way_Back "The Way, Way Back")*. The bridge features prominently in the film *[The Equalizer 2](/wiki/The_Equalizer_2#Filming "The Equalizer 2")*.
* Tree of Knowledge Corner: A tri-point intersection between Elm Street, Summer Street, and South Street, where mail carriers used to drop off their mail inboxes nailed to a tree.[[48]](#cite_note-48) The tree is no longer standing as it was hit by lightning but in its place is an engraved stone.
